question: Define what it means for a family of subsets in a metric space to have the finite intersection property. State the theorem that relates this property to compactness.
expectedPoints: A family of sets has the finite intersection property if every finite subcollection has a non-empty intersection.
A metric space is compact if and only if every family of closed sets with the finite intersection property has a non-empty intersection.
This theorem provides an alternative characterization of compactness using closed sets and intersections.

question: Explain, using the concept of the finite intersection property, why a nested sequence of non-empty closed subsets in a compact metric space has a non-empty intersection.
expectedPoints: A nested sequence (decreasing by inclusion) of non-empty closed sets automatically has the finite intersection property, as any finite intersection is just the smallest set in that finite subcollection, which is non-empty.
Since the space is compact, the theorem states that the entire family (the sequence) must have a non-empty intersection.
This is a crucial application of the finite intersection property and is often used to prove results like the Cantor Intersection Theorem.

question: Prove that a metric space X is compact if and only if every family of closed subsets of X with the finite intersection property satisfies that the intersection of the entire family is non-empty.
expectedPoints: For the forward direction: Assume X is compact. Let {F_α} be a family of closed sets with the FIP. Suppose, for contradiction, that ∩F_α = ∅. Then the complements {F_α^c} form an open cover of X. By compactness, a finite subcover exists, meaning F_{α1}^c ∪ ... ∪ F_{αn}^c = X. Taking complements, this implies F_{α1} ∩ ... ∩ F_{αn} = ∅, contradicting the FIP.

For the reverse direction: Assume the FIP condition holds. To prove X is compact, let {U_β} be an open cover. Suppose, for contradiction, no finite subcover exists. Then the family of closed sets {F_β} = {U_β^c} has the FIP, because the failure of a finite subcover means any finite intersection of these complements is non-empty. By our assumption, ∩F_β ≠ ∅. This means ∩U_β^c ≠ ∅, so the union of the U_β (the open cover) is not equal to X, which is a contradiction.
The proof highlights the duality between open covers and families of closed sets via complements, and is a fundamental result in the theory of compactness.
